title: Setup Guide PL/I & CICS
description: Learn how to set up your development environment for PL/I and CICS.

theory.markdown:
## Setup: connecting your workstation to z/OS (so you can actually code)

### Why setup is part of the skill
**Reality check:** on IBM Z, productive work depends on getting the access path right — credentials, host endpoint, and the interface you use. Setup issues often look like “tool bugs,” but are usually misconfiguration or missing authority.

### What “a working setup” means (definition you can test)
Minimum success criteria:
- you can authenticate successfully
- you can browse the resources you’re supposed to use (data sets / members / USS files)
- you can open/edit members without errors
- you can submit a job and view output

A login that works but shows empty lists often means **authority** (permissions) rather than a broken tool.

### The z/OS resource mental model (what you’re really navigating)
Before tools, understand what exists on the host:
- **Data sets** (like files) and **members** (like files inside a library)
- **Jobs** (submitted work) and **spool output** (results)
- **USS files** (Unix System Services filesystem)

If you know these nouns, every tool UI becomes easier.

### Two common interaction styles (both are real)
- **Workstation-first (VS Code + extensions)**: edit/browse through a modern IDE while connecting to z/OS services.
- **Host-first (TSO/ISPF)**: work directly in the classic panel-based environment on z/OS.

### Common setup failures (and what they usually mean)
- **Cannot connect**: host/port wrong, VPN/network missing, service not available
- **Can connect but no data sets**: profile points to wrong HLQ, or missing authority
- **Can edit but cannot run/submit**: missing permissions for job submission or required services
- **Can submit but no output**: job ended quickly, output class mismatch, or you’re looking in the wrong place

### Practical “first success” checklist
- verify you can browse a known data set
- open a member
- make a small edit
- submit something minimal
- find the output

That loop is enough to unlock the rest of the course.
theory.content:
Welcome to IBM Explorer for z/OS Setup

Welcome to this setup guide for IBM Explorer for z/OS and for connecting to your z/OS environment through either Visual Studio Code or ISPF. This section is designed to help you understand the role of IBM Explorer for z/OS, why it is useful in a mainframe workflow, and what you need to prepare before you begin working with data sets, members, UNIX System Services files, or batch jobs on IBM Z. A good setup is one of the most important parts of getting started, because even simple tasks become frustrating when the client tools, host connection details, or permissions are not configured correctly.

Many beginners assume that the hardest part of mainframe work is learning commands or writing code, but in practice the first real challenge is often making sure the environment is connected properly. You need to know which interface you are expected to use, how your organization exposes access to z/OS, and whether you will work through a graphical workstation tool, a modern code editor, or a traditional terminal-based interface. This guide introduces those paths and explains how they relate to each other so that the rest of your learning or development experience starts on a solid foundation.

What IBM Explorer for z/OS Is

IBM Explorer for z/OS, often also called z/OS Explorer, is a workstation-based tool that provides a more visual and organized way to interact with IBM Z systems. Instead of relying only on traditional terminal screens, it gives users a desktop-style interface for managing host connections, browsing resources, and working with artifacts that live on the z/OS system. In a typical learning or enterprise environment, it acts as a bridge between the developer workstation and the mainframe host.

Its purpose is not to replace the z/OS platform itself, but to make access to that platform more manageable. Through IBM Explorer for z/OS, users can often view and organize data sets, inspect members, interact with remote resources, and work with projects in a way that feels more familiar to people used to desktop development tools. This is especially useful in training environments and modern enterprise workflows where users need an easier entry point into a powerful but complex system.

Understanding this tool matters because it helps explain the bigger setup picture. IBM Explorer for z/OS is not just another editor. It sits within the larger z/OS access workflow and helps define how users connect, organize resources, and interact with the host. Once you understand that role, it becomes much easier to understand why VS Code can be used in some environments, why ISPF is still important in others, and why connection setup always comes before productive work.

Why the Setup Matters

A correct setup matters because access to z/OS depends on several pieces working together at the same time. The local workstation has to be ready, the correct client or extension has to be installed, the host details have to be entered properly, and the user account must have the required permissions. If only one of those parts is missing or incorrect, even a simple action such as opening a member or connecting to a profile can fail. That is why setup should not be treated as a quick preliminary step. It is the base of the entire workflow.

For learners, this is especially important because setup problems often look like application problems. A failed connection may appear to be a tooling issue when the real cause is a wrong port or missing service. An empty list of resources may look like a broken editor when the actual problem is insufficient authority. A session that refuses to start may appear to be a user mistake when the host-side access path was never enabled. Taking the time to understand the setup process helps avoid this confusion and gives you a clearer mental model of how workstation tools connect to the mainframe.

Setting Up the VS Code Connection

In many modern environments, users work with z/OS through Visual Studio Code. This approach is popular because it combines a familiar editing experience with extensions that allow access to mainframe resources. Instead of doing everything through classic terminal panels, users can work with source files and host resources in a desktop editor that supports navigation, organization, and extension-based workflows. This makes VS Code a practical option for students, developers, and teams that want a more modern interface while still connecting to the IBM Z platform.

When using VS Code for z/OS access, the setup usually depends on installing the right extensions and defining a valid connection profile. The editor itself does not automatically know how to reach a mainframe. It needs the proper tooling layer that allows it to browse host resources, communicate with system services, and authenticate against the target environment. That means the real work is not only installing VS Code, but also preparing the connection path that links your workstation to the z/OS system.

A complete VS Code setup generally involves confirming that the required extensions are installed, checking that the host name and port values are correct, validating the authentication method used by your environment, and making sure your user account has access to the parts of the system you need. Once that is working, VS Code can be used to browse remote resources, open members, work with files in a more flexible editor, and interact with the system in a way that feels much more natural for users coming from non-mainframe development backgrounds.

The biggest advantage of this path is usability. VS Code provides a clean editing experience, a recognizable file-oriented layout, and a workflow that many developers already know. This lowers the entry barrier for working with z/OS while still keeping the connection to real enterprise resources. For many users, this becomes the preferred daily interface once the connection is configured correctly.

Using ISPF Instead

ISPF is the traditional interactive interface used by many z/OS users to work directly on the mainframe. Unlike VS Code, which provides a modern desktop-based editing experience, ISPF is panel-driven and keyboard-oriented. It is widely used for browsing and editing data set members, navigating libraries, running utilities, and working within the classic TSO/ISPF environment. In many organizations, it remains one of the most important and most dependable ways to access z/OS resources.

Choosing ISPF does not mean using an outdated workflow without value. It means using an interface that has been central to real operational work on IBM Z for a long time. Many experienced users prefer it because it is direct, efficient, and closely tied to the structure of the host environment. It also teaches an important part of mainframe culture and practice, because it exposes the user more directly to how data sets, members, commands, and utilities are organized on the system.

When your setup path uses ISPF, the focus is less about workstation extensions and more about making sure you can successfully sign in to the correct terminal or emulator session and reach the TSO/ISPF environment. Your credentials must be valid, your host access must be active, and your account must be allowed to use the datasets or utilities required for your work. Once that access is working, ISPF becomes a powerful environment for editing and managing resources directly on the mainframe.

VS Code and ISPF in the Same Workflow

These two approaches are often presented as alternatives, but in practice they can also complement each other. VS Code is useful when you want a more modern editing experience, easier navigation, and a familiar development environment on your workstation. ISPF is useful when you want direct access to the traditional host interface and the utilities that many mainframe workflows still depend on. Learning both is often beneficial because it gives you flexibility and helps you understand the z/OS environment from more than one angle.

A user might prefer VS Code for general editing and browsing, but still use ISPF for certain system-oriented tasks, dataset operations, or traditional workflows taught in training material. Another user might begin in ISPF to understand the basics of host navigation and later move into VS Code for convenience. IBM Explorer for z/OS fits naturally into this picture because it helps represent the workstation side of z/OS access and reinforces the idea that mainframe work is not tied to only one interface style.

What You Should Have Ready Before Starting

Before beginning the setup, it is important to make sure you have the essential information and access details ready. In most cases, this includes the host name or IP address of the z/OS system, the correct port or service endpoint, the user credentials you were given, and confirmation of which connection method your environment expects. Without these details, the setup process usually stops very early, even if the client software itself has been installed correctly.

You should also know which workflow you are expected to use. Some environments are built around VS Code and extension-based access. Others are designed around terminal access and ISPF. Some training environments support both. Knowing this in advance helps prevent wasted time and makes it easier to follow the correct instructions from the start. It also helps you understand which tools are mandatory and which tools are optional.

If possible, verify early that your account has permission to access the resources needed for your course or project. A successful sign-in alone is not always enough. You may still need authority to browse certain datasets, open specific files, or use particular services. Checking that early prevents confusion later, especially when the connection seems correct but the environment still appears incomplete.

What This Setup Guide Will Help You Do

By the end of the setup process, the goal is to have a stable and usable connection to your z/OS environment. That means you should be able to launch the correct client or interface, authenticate successfully, reach the host system, and begin interacting with the resources assigned to you. Whether you choose the workstation-oriented path through VS Code or the traditional path through ISPF, the desired result is the same: a working environment that allows you to start real work instead of spending time fixing access problems.

Once the setup is complete, everything else becomes easier. You can focus on learning how the system is organized, how data sets and members are managed, and how daily tasks are performed in your environment. That is why this introduction begins with IBM Explorer for z/OS and connection setup. It is not just a technical prerequisite. It is the foundation that supports the rest of the workflow.

theory.markdown:
## VS Code mainframe extensions and ISPF: two ways into the same system

### What you are really choosing
**Key idea:** VS Code + extensions and ISPF are two interfaces to the *same* underlying z/OS resources (data sets, members, jobs, USS files). The difference is the interaction style and where the tool runs.

- **VS Code** runs on your workstation and connects to z/OS services.
- **ISPF** is the classic panel-based environment on the host side (TSO/ISPF).

### VS Code path (modern workstation workflow)
What you get:
- a connection/profile (host, service/port, credentials)
- dataset/member browsing
- job browsing and spool viewing
- language-aware editing (syntax + formatting)

**Practical strength:** it’s easier to learn because it looks like a modern editor.

### ISPF path (host-native workflow)
What you get:
- fast panel navigation
- direct dataset utilities
- mature host-native editing and workflows

**Practical strength:** it teaches how the host is organized and how experienced teams actually work.

### How to think about “skills you must learn” (tool-independent)
No matter which UI you use, you must learn:
- what a data set is
- how members are named and organized
- how to submit a job
- how to find the output

### Practical: making sure your environment is ready for coding exercises
If you can do these, you can complete almost every practical question:
- open the source member
- edit and save
- compile/run (submit)
- inspect output and fix errors

### Quiz readiness (what you must remember)
- The first step is typically **installing the required tooling (extensions) / configuring the environment** before you can do real work.
theory.content:
Welcome to VS Code Mainframe Extensions and ISPF

This section introduces the two main environments many learners encounter when starting with IBM Z development and navigation: Visual Studio Code with mainframe extensions, and ISPF on z/OS itself. Although they look very different, both are used to access and work with the same kinds of enterprise resources, such as data sets, members, jobs, and UNIX System Services files. Understanding how they differ helps you build a clearer picture of how modern and traditional mainframe workflows fit together.

For many beginners, the biggest challenge is not the syntax of a language or the logic of a program, but learning how to move around the environment confidently. That is why it is useful to begin with the tools themselves. VS Code offers a modern workstation-based experience that feels familiar to many developers, while ISPF gives direct access to the classic interactive interface that has been central to z/OS work for decades. Learning both gives you both convenience and context.

What the VS Code Mainframe Extensions Are

In a modern workstation workflow, VS Code becomes a front end for working with mainframe resources through specialized extensions. One of the most important of these is IBM Z Open Editor, which brings enterprise language support into Visual Studio Code. Instead of treating mainframe work as something that must happen only in traditional host interfaces, it allows developers to use a familiar editor while still working with real z/OS resources and source content.

Alongside IBM Z Open Editor, Zowe Explorer plays a major role in the VS Code workflow. It provides the views and connection layer that let users interact with z/OS resources from within the editor. This includes browsing data sets and members, accessing UNIX System Services files, and viewing jobs and output. Together, these extensions make VS Code much more than a plain text editor. They turn it into a practical environment for navigating and editing mainframe resources from a desktop interface.

This matters because it lowers the entry barrier for new developers. A user who is already comfortable with VS Code can stay in a development environment that feels modern and recognizable while still connecting to a z/OS system. That makes the first steps into mainframe development less intimidating and often more productive.

Why VS Code Is Useful in a Mainframe Workflow

The value of VS Code in a mainframe workflow comes from the combination of familiarity and flexibility. Many developers already know how to work in Visual Studio Code, so they can focus on learning z/OS concepts instead of first learning an unfamiliar editing environment. Features such as structured navigation, extension support, and a cleaner workstation experience make it easier to organize work and move between files and resources.

Another important benefit is that VS Code supports a more modern way of thinking about enterprise development. It helps connect traditional host resources to contemporary editing practices, which is valuable in organizations that are modernizing their development workflows without abandoning proven systems. Rather than forcing every user into the same traditional interface from the beginning, it creates an additional path that is often easier to adopt.

At the same time, using VS Code for mainframe work does not remove the need to understand the host environment. The editor is only one part of the workflow. A successful setup still depends on proper connection details, authentication, and permission to access the z/OS resources required for your course, lab, or project. In other words, VS Code makes access more user-friendly, but it still depends on the structure and rules of the mainframe system underneath.

What ISPF Is

ISPF, short for Interactive System Productivity Facility, is one of the classic interactive environments on z/OS. It is panel-based, keyboard-driven, and closely tied to the traditional way many users work directly on the mainframe. Unlike VS Code, which runs on the workstation and connects outward to z/OS, ISPF is part of the host-side experience itself and is used for tasks such as browsing libraries, editing members, running utilities, and navigating development-related options.

ISPF remains important because it is not just a legacy interface that survived by accident. It has been central to real operational work for a very long time and is still widely used because it is efficient, direct, and deeply integrated into everyday z/OS practice. Many experienced users rely on it because it provides quick access to data sets, utilities, and commands without requiring a separate workstation-driven workflow.

For learners, ISPF is valuable because it reveals more of the structure of the host environment. It helps you understand how libraries are organized, how members are edited, how utilities are run, and how traditional mainframe interaction works at a practical level. Even if you later prefer VS Code for day-to-day editing, knowing ISPF gives you a stronger understanding of how z/OS work is actually performed.

How VS Code and ISPF Compare

The biggest difference between the two environments is the style of interaction. VS Code is a modern graphical editor running on your workstation, extended with tooling that lets it reach and manage mainframe resources. ISPF is a traditional host-oriented interface navigated mainly through panels, menus, and keyboard commands. One emphasizes workstation familiarity and extension-based tooling, while the other emphasizes direct interaction with the z/OS environment itself.

That difference does not mean one is automatically better than the other. VS Code is often easier for beginners to approach, especially if they already have experience with modern code editors. ISPF, however, often provides deeper exposure to the classic operational style of the platform and remains highly useful for traditional workflows. In practice, many users benefit from understanding both. VS Code can improve comfort and productivity, while ISPF builds a stronger understanding of the host system and its native way of working.

Why It Helps to Learn Both

Learning both environments gives you flexibility. You may prefer VS Code for its editing comfort, but still need ISPF for exercises, utilities, or workflows that are taught from the host perspective. You may begin with ISPF to understand the traditional structure of z/OS and later move into VS Code for a more convenient editing experience. Either path is valid, and each environment teaches something different.

More importantly, studying both shows that mainframe work is not limited to one interface style. Modern IBM Z development can include workstation-based tools, extension-driven editing, and contemporary workflows, while still relying on traditional interfaces that remain powerful and relevant. That balance between continuity and modernization is one of the defining features of the mainframe world, and VS Code together with ISPF is a good example of that balance in practice.

What This Section Prepares You For

By understanding the role of the VS Code mainframe extensions and the purpose of ISPF, you prepare yourself for the next step in the learning process: actually connecting, navigating, and working with z/OS resources. Once you know what each environment is designed to do, setup instructions become easier to follow and the overall workflow becomes easier to understand. Instead of seeing the tools as isolated pieces, you begin to see them as different entry points into the same enterprise platform.

That is the main goal of this introduction. It gives you the conceptual foundation needed before you move into installation, profile configuration, host access, and practical usage. With that foundation in place, the tools feel less confusing and the mainframe environment becomes much easier to approach with confidence.

exercise.instructions: Write a simple PL/I program to verify your setup is working correctly.
exercise.hint: Create a basic 'Hello World' program using PROC OPTIONS(MAIN).

questions.question: What is the first step in setting up PL/I?
